title: Hypertensive Emergency: Recognition and Management
objectives: Apply specific BP targets for different scenarios

sections.title: Definitions: Emergency vs Urgency
sections.content:
**Hypertensive Emergency:**
• Severely elevated BP (typically >180/120 mmHg)
• WITH acute target organ damage
• Requires immediate IV therapy and ICU admission

**Hypertensive Urgency:**
• Severely elevated BP (typically >180/120 mmHg)
• WITHOUT acute target organ damage
• Can be treated with oral agents, close outpatient follow-up

**Key Point:**
The NUMBER doesn't define an emergency—the presence of END-ORGAN DAMAGE does. A BP of 220/130 without symptoms may be urgency, while 180/110 with encephalopathy is an emergency.

sections.title: Target Organ Damage Patterns
sections.content:
**Neurological:**
• Hypertensive encephalopathy (headache, confusion, visual changes, seizures)
• Ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke
• Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)

**Cardiovascular:**
• Acute coronary syndrome
• Acute heart failure/pulmonary edema
• Aortic dissection

**Renal:**
• Acute kidney injury
• Hematuria, proteinuria
• Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia

**Ophthalmologic:**
• Papilledema
• Flame hemorrhages
• Cotton wool spots
• Vision loss

**Obstetric:**
• Eclampsia/severe preeclampsia
• HELLP syndrome

sections.title: Initial Evaluation
sections.content:
**History:**
• Duration and severity of hypertension
• Antihypertensive medication compliance
• Symptoms of end-organ damage (chest pain, dyspnea, neurological symptoms)
• Drug use (cocaine, amphetamines, MAOIs)

**Physical Exam:**
• BP in both arms (aortic dissection screen)
• Fundoscopic exam (papilledema, hemorrhages)
• Cardiac exam (S3, murmurs)
• Neurological exam (mental status, focal deficits)

**Laboratory:**
• BMP (creatinine, electrolytes)
• Urinalysis (protein, RBCs)
• CBC with peripheral smear (MAHA)
• Troponin, BNP if cardiac symptoms

**Imaging:**
• ECG (LVH, ischemia)
• Chest X-ray (pulmonary edema, widened mediastinum)
• CT head if neurological symptoms
• CT angiography if dissection suspected

sections.title: General Treatment Principles
sections.content:
**Goals:**
1. Lower BP to reduce ongoing damage
2. Avoid excessive drops that cause hypoperfusion
3. Use IV titratable agents for control

**Default Target:**
• Reduce MAP by 20-25% in first hour
• Then to 160/100-110 over next 2-6 hours
• Then gradual normalization over 24-48 hours

**Why not drop BP too fast?**
Chronic hypertension shifts the cerebral autoregulation curve rightward. Rapid BP drops can cause watershed infarcts, especially in the elderly.

**Exceptions to gradual lowering:**
• Aortic dissection: Rapid, aggressive lowering
• Ischemic stroke with thrombolysis candidate: Specific targets
• Preeclampsia/eclampsia: Prevent seizures

sections.title: IV Antihypertensive Agents
sections.content:
**Nicardipine:**
• Start: 5 mg/hr IV, titrate by 2.5 mg/hr every 5-15 min
• Max: 15 mg/hr
• Good for: Most emergencies, stroke, renal-safe
• Avoid in: Acute HF (negative inotropy)

**Labetalol:**
• Bolus: 20 mg IV, then 20-80 mg every 10 min (max 300 mg)
• Infusion: 0.5-2 mg/min
• Good for: Aortic dissection, ACS, eclampsia
• Avoid in: Acute HF, bradycardia, asthma, cocaine

**Clevidipine:**
• Start: 1-2 mg/hr, titrate by doubling every 90 sec
• Max: 32 mg/hr
• Very titratable, ultra-short half-life
• Lipid emulsion—caution in pancreatitis

**Nitroprusside:**
• Start: 0.3-0.5 mcg/kg/min
• Max: 10 mcg/kg/min (cyanide toxicity)
• Very potent, requires arterial line
• Avoid prolonged use (>48-72 hrs)

**Esmolol:**
• Load: 500 mcg/kg over 1 min
• Infusion: 50-200 mcg/kg/min
• Ultra-short acting beta-blocker
• Good for: Aortic dissection, perioperative

**Hydralazine:**
• 10-20 mg IV every 4-6 hours
• Unpredictable, reflex tachycardia
• Traditional for pregnancy (magnesium for seizure prevention)

sections.title: Scenario-Specific Management
sections.content:
**Aortic Dissection:**
• Target: SBP <120 AND HR <60 in 20 minutes
• FIRST: Beta-blocker (esmolol or labetalol)
• THEN: Vasodilator if needed (nicardipine, nitroprusside)
• Vasodilator alone causes reflex tachycardia → ↑ shear stress

**Acute Ischemic Stroke:**
• If thrombolysis candidate: <185/110 before, <180/105 after
• If NOT receiving thrombolysis: Treat only if >220/120
• Avoid rapid drops (risk of infarct extension)

**Intracerebral Hemorrhage:**
• Target SBP 140-180 mmHg
• Aggressive lowering (<140) may increase mortality

**Acute Pulmonary Edema:**
• Nitroglycerin + furosemide
• Avoid pure beta-blockers (negative inotropy)

**Preeclampsia/Eclampsia:**
• Labetalol or hydralazine
• Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis
• Delivery is definitive treatment

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
sections.content:
**1. Treating the number, not the patient:**
Asymptomatic severe hypertension in a non-compliant patient may just need restart of oral meds and follow-up.

**2. Using sublingual nifedipine:**
Causes unpredictable, precipitous BP drops → stroke, MI. Never use.

**3. Dropping BP too fast:**
Especially dangerous in elderly, chronic hypertensives, and stroke patients.

**4. Missing secondary causes:**
• Renal artery stenosis (especially if ACEi-induced AKI)
• Pheochromocytoma (paroxysmal, catecholamine symptoms)
• Drug-induced (cocaine, amphetamines, MAOI + tyramine)

**5. Forgetting pain and anxiety:**
Treating pain and anxiety often lowers BP significantly.

keyTakeaways: End-organ damage, not BP number, defines a hypertensive emergency
Default: Lower MAP by 20-25% in first hour, then gradual reduction
Aortic dissection requires aggressive, rapid BP AND heart rate control
Never use sublingual nifedipine—unpredictable and dangerous
Ischemic stroke has permissive hypertension unless thrombolysis planned
Always look for secondary causes in resistant or paroxysmal hypertension

references.citation: Whelton PK, Carey RM, Aronow WS, et al. 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2018;71(19):e127-e248
references.keyContent: Comprehensive HTN guideline including emergency management

references.citation: van den Born BJ, Lip GYH, Brguljan-Hitij J, et al. ESC Council on Hypertension Position Document on Management of Hypertensive Emergencies. Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Pharmacother. 2019;5(1):37-46
references.keyContent: European consensus on IV agent selection and BP targets
End-organ damage patterns, IV medication dosing

references.citation: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al. Guidelines for the Early Management of Acute Ischemic Stroke. Stroke. 2019;50:e344-e418

reviewQuestions.question: A 58-year-old with BP 210/130 has severe headache, confusion, and papilledema. What is the initial BP target?
options: Reduce MAP by 20-25% in first hour
reviewQuestions.explanation: This is hypertensive encephalopathy (emergency). The goal is to reduce MAP by 20-25% in the first hour to prevent ongoing damage while avoiding excessive drops that could cause watershed ischemia.

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with acute aortic dissection has BP 190/110 and HR 95. What is the correct first-line treatment?
reviewQuestions.explanation: In aortic dissection, beta-blocker must be given FIRST to lower heart rate and reduce shear stress. Vasodilators alone cause reflex tachycardia, worsening aortic wall stress. Target: SBP <120 AND HR <60.

reviewQuestions.question: A patient presenting with acute ischemic stroke has BP 195/105 and is a thrombolysis candidate. What is the BP target before tPA?
reviewQuestions.explanation: For thrombolysis candidates, BP must be <185/110 before tPA and maintained <180/105 for 24 hours after. Higher BP increases hemorrhagic transformation risk.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected hypertensive emergency?

options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in hypertensive emergency. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination

coachDx.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Obtain a thorough history and physical examination from Consult a specialist before any workup.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of hypertensive emergency. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically

coachDx.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Consider the differential diagnosis systematically from Order every available test to rule out all possibilities.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.

mcqs.stem: When managing hypertensive emergency, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values

coachDx.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values from Always defer to the most recent published study.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.

mcqs.stem: An ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL. Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?

options.explanation: Left main occlusion causes widespread ST depression with ST elevation in aVR — a pattern of diffuse subendocardial ischemia, not isolated inferior STEMI.

ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal changes in lateral leads (I, aVL) localizes to an inferior STEMI, most commonly caused by RCA occlusion (~85% of cases). Check right-sided leads (V4R) to assess for RV involvement.
LCx causes lateral STEMI (I, aVL, V5-V6). While LCx can cause inferior changes in ~15% of cases (dominant LCx), RCA is far more common.
LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in V1-V4 (± V5-V6), not inferior leads.

correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.

coachDx.misconception: Left main occlusion causes widespread ST depression with ST elevation in aVR — a pattern of diffuse subendocardial ischemia, not isolated inferior STEMI.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Right coronary artery (RCA) from Left main coronary artery.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Right coronary artery (RCA).
